title: First Opium War begins
summary: In 1839, the Qing imperial commissioner Lin Zexu marched into the foreign factories at Canton, confiscated more than twenty thousand chests of British opium and, over three summer weeks at Humen, dissolved them in lime and water. The British government, urged on by merchants and by the political weight of the free-trade lobby, responded by sending an expeditionary force. The war that followed, ended in 1842 by the Treaty of Nanking, gave Britain Hong Kong Island, opened five treaty ports, imposed a large indemnity, and inaugurated the century that Chinese nationalists later called the age of national humiliation.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
For most of the eighteenth century the great imbalance of the China trade had run the other way. British consumers paid for Chinese tea, silk and porcelain with silver bullion because Chinese buyers had little interest in British woollens or Indian cotton. From the 1760s onward the East India Company found a solution in Bengal opium. The Company's monopoly on cultivation in the Ganges valley produced a standardised chest of forty kilograms; independent merchant houses, known as country traders, carried the chests to the south China coast and sold them for silver, which then paid for the tea. By the 1830s British imports of Chinese tea had passed thirty million pounds a year and Indian opium exports to China had risen from about two hundred chests in 1729 to some forty thousand chests by 1838.

The Qing court had prohibited opium smoking as early as 1729 and its importation in 1796, but enforcement was undermined by the corruption of the coastal officials and the vast profits of the traffic. By the late 1830s the drain of silver from China was severe enough to disrupt tax payments in the provinces, and the number of habitual smokers, estimated by contemporary officials in the millions, alarmed the Daoguang Emperor. A great court debate in 1836 pitted a legalising faction, which wanted to tax and regulate the drug, against a suppression faction that demanded rigorous prohibition. The suppressionists won. On 31 December 1838 the emperor appointed Lin Zexu, the fifty-three-year-old governor-general of Hunan and Hubei and one of the most respected officials in the empire, imperial commissioner for the suppression of the opium trade at Canton.

Lin arrived in Canton on 10 March 1839. Within days he arrested Chinese opium dealers, issued proclamations to the foreign merchants demanding the surrender of all opium in their warehouses on ships in the harbour, and ordered the Cohong merchants to explain themselves. When the traders temporised, Lin surrounded the factories of the foreign community with troops, cut off food and servants, and confined about three hundred and fifty foreigners, including the British superintendent of trade, Captain Charles Elliot, for six weeks. Elliot, without authority from London, promised the merchants that the British government would reimburse them, and ordered them to surrender the drug. Between 3 April and 21 May 1839 the traders handed over 20,283 chests, some 1,200 tonnes of opium, worth more than two million pounds sterling. Lin destroyed the stock over twenty-three days at Humen, mixing it with lime and salt in great trenches and washing the sludge out to sea. In a moral gesture he wrote a letter to Queen Victoria appealing to her Christian conscience; it was never delivered.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
In London the news arrived in September 1839. Elliot's guarantee of compensation transformed a private dispute into a public liability of two million pounds, which the Whig government of Lord Melbourne was unwilling to pay from the exchequer. The foreign secretary, Lord Palmerston, chose instead to make China pay, and to open the country to the free trade that British and Indian merchants had long demanded. In April 1840 the House of Commons debated the war in the celebrated speech in which the young Tory William Gladstone denounced 'a war more unjust in its origin, a war more calculated in its progress to cover this country with permanent disgrace, I do not know'. The government won the vote by nine votes and dispatched an expeditionary force of some sixteen warships and four thousand soldiers under Rear-Admiral George Elliot, the superintendent's cousin, with orders to blockade the coast, seize a defensible island, and dictate terms.

The fleet reached Macao in June 1840. Ignoring Canton, whose defences under Lin were formidable, it sailed north, blockaded the mouth of the Yangzi, captured the port of Dinghai on Zhoushan Island on 5 July, and continued to the Peiho estuary near Tianjin to deliver Palmerston's demands to the Qing court. The alarmed Daoguang Emperor dismissed Lin and sent the Manchu grandee Qishan to negotiate. In January 1841 Qishan and Charles Elliot, now sole plenipotentiary, produced the Convention of Chuenpi, which ceded Hong Kong Island, paid an indemnity of six million dollars and opened direct trade. Neither government would ratify it. The emperor was furious at the surrender of imperial territory; Palmerston was furious at the meagre commercial gains. Both plenipotentiaries were sacked.

Elliot was replaced by Sir Henry Pottinger, the naval command passed to Vice-Admiral William Parker, and the army was reinforced under Major-General Hugh Gough. In the second campaign of 1841 and 1842 the British used steam-powered iron gunboats, most famously the Nemesis, to work up the coast and into the great rivers where the Chinese junk fleets and shore batteries could not follow. Amoy fell on 26 August 1841, Ningbo on 13 October, Zhapu on 18 May 1842, Shanghai on 19 June, and the great Grand Canal city of Zhenjiang on 21 July after a bitter fight in which the Manchu bannermen killed their own families rather than surrender. With British warships at the Yangzi mouth, ready to cut the grain tribute route to Beijing, the court sued for terms. On 29 August 1842 the Treaty of Nanking was signed on the deck of HMS Cornwallis. It ceded Hong Kong Island to Britain in perpetuity, opened the five treaty ports of Canton, Amoy, Foochow, Ningbo and Shanghai to British residence and trade, imposed a twenty-one million silver dollar indemnity for the destroyed opium, the merchants' debts and the cost of the war, and abolished the Cohong monopoly. British casualties across the war were remarkably light, some sixty-nine killed in action; Chinese losses ran into the tens of thousands.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The Treaty of Nanking was the first of what Chinese historians would call the unequal treaties. A supplementary Treaty of the Bogue in 1843 added extraterritoriality, by which British subjects in China were tried by British consuls under British law, and most-favoured-nation status, which meant that any concession granted to another power became automatically British. The United States negotiated the Treaty of Wangxia in 1844 and France the Treaty of Whampoa the same year, both on the same terms. The Qing state, which had for two centuries treated foreign trade as a matter of tribute and imperial grace, now found itself bound by international treaties that constrained its sovereignty over tariffs, jurisdiction and residence.

The opium trade itself, which had triggered the war, was not banned by the treaty. It continued, tolerated in practice by Chinese and British authorities alike, and grew steadily until it was formally legalised at the end of the Second Opium War in 1858. Hong Kong Island, ceded almost as an afterthought, became within a decade one of the great commercial harbours of Asia and remained under British rule until 1997.

The deeper consequences for China were political and psychological. The exposure of the Qing's military weakness set off a cascade of internal crises. The Taiping Rebellion, launched in Guangxi in 1850 by the millenarian Hong Xiuquan, would kill an estimated twenty to thirty million people over the following fifteen years and devastate the richest provinces of the empire. The Nian Rebellion in the north, the Muslim rebellions in the northwest and southwest, and the Second Opium War of 1856 to 1860 followed in quick succession. Reforming officials such as Zeng Guofan, Li Hongzhang and Zuo Zongtang launched the Self-Strengthening Movement to modernise the army and navy and to build arsenals and shipyards, but the deeper structures of the Qing state proved resistant to reform. The dynasty would survive until the revolution of 1911.

For Britain the war confirmed the Royal Navy as the arbiter of the world's oceans and opened the last great market to European commerce. For India it entrenched the Bengal opium monopoly as one of the pillars of Company revenue, a fact that made the drug trade a permanent political question in London. And it demonstrated a formula, gunboat, treaty, treaty port, that European powers would repeat from Japan to Zanzibar for the rest of the century.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The First Opium War is remembered in China as the beginning of the bainian guochi, the century of national humiliation that stretched from 1839 to the founding of the People's Republic in 1949. Every generation of Chinese schoolchildren learns the story of Lin Zexu at Humen, the burning of the Old Summer Palace by Anglo-French troops in 1860, the imposition of the treaty ports, the loss of Taiwan to Japan in 1895 and the concessions extracted after the Boxer Rising in 1901 as episodes of a single narrative of imperial predation. Lin Zexu, whose downfall in the autumn of 1840 sent him to exile in the western frontier province of Xinjiang, has been rehabilitated as one of the first modern Chinese statesmen: a Confucian scholar-official who saw the Western threat clearly, tried to defend his country and its people from the drug, and lost through the failures of others.

The legacy in international law is nearly as important. The treaty port system, extraterritoriality, most-favoured-nation clauses and fixed low tariffs became for the rest of the nineteenth century the template of European informal empire, applied in Egypt, the Ottoman Empire, Japan (after 1858), Siam and elsewhere. Their gradual abolition, largely completed only in the mid-twentieth century, became one of the central objects of nationalist politics across Asia. Hong Kong, ceded in 1842, retroceded in 1997, remains a physical monument to the war.

In Britain the war continues to be debated by historians. The evangelical and radical opposition that Gladstone voiced in 1840 was the first widespread expression of moral doubt about the imperial project, and the Anti-Opium Society founded in 1874 by British reformers made the trade a domestic scandal until the Anglo-Chinese agreements of 1907 finally began the phase-out. For historians of globalisation, the war marks the moment when the industrial economies of the West, propelled by steam power, iron ships and rifled guns, could impose their terms on the older commercial empires of Asia and rewrite the rules of the world market.

worldContext.text: The United States in 1839 was in the middle of the panic that had followed Andrew Jackson's dismantling of the Second Bank of the United States. In November the Amistad, a Cuban slave ship whose captive Africans had risen in mutiny, was seized off Long Island by the US Navy; the resulting case, argued before the Supreme Court by former president John Quincy Adams, would free the survivors in 1841. In Texas the young Republic under President Mirabeau Lamar was pursuing an expansionist policy that pointed toward eventual annexation to the United States.

British India was in the opening phase of the disastrous First Anglo-Afghan War. In August 1839 an Army of the Indus under Sir John Keane occupied Kabul and installed the puppet Shah Shuja on the throne; the uprising against the occupation in November 1841 would culminate in the annihilation of General William Elphinstone's retreating column of some sixteen thousand troops and camp followers in the mountain passes east of Kabul in January 1842, one of the great disasters of British imperial history.

The Ottoman Empire was in the Second Egyptian-Ottoman War. On 24 June 1839 the modernised Egyptian army of Muhammad Ali's son Ibrahim Pasha destroyed the Ottoman army at the Battle of Nezib in southeastern Anatolia; the Ottoman sultan Mahmud II died a week later without knowing of the defeat. The Ottoman fleet defected to Alexandria. Only the intervention of the European great powers, formalised in the Convention of London of 1840, saved the empire and forced Muhammad Ali to accept hereditary rule of Egypt in exchange for withdrawal from Syria.

In southern Africa the Great Trek of Boer settlers out of the British Cape Colony into the interior had reached its climax. On 16 December 1838, at the Battle of Blood River, a Voortrekker commando under Andries Pretorius defeated a Zulu army of perhaps ten to fifteen thousand under Dingane; the Voortrekker republic of Natalia was proclaimed in 1839 and would be annexed by Britain in 1843. In West Africa the Sokoto Caliphate remained the largest state, and Portuguese, French and British anti-slave-trade squadrons patrolled the coasts.

Britain in 1839 was in the first mass ferment of the Chartist movement. On 12 July the House of Commons rejected the first Chartist petition, signed by 1.28 million people, and a rising in Newport in November was suppressed with the loss of about twenty-two lives. The young Queen Victoria, on the throne since June 1837, faced the Bedchamber Crisis in May. On the continent Louis-Philippe's July Monarchy in France attempted to stabilise its finances while the German states were beginning the tariff and railway boom that would transform the Zollverein economy.

In New Zealand the increasingly disorderly presence of British whalers, traders and land speculators, together with the arrival of the New Zealand Company under Edward Gibbon Wakefield in 1839, pushed the colonial office in London to intervene. Captain William Hobson was dispatched as consul in July 1839 with instructions to negotiate a treaty with Maori chiefs; the resulting Treaty of Waitangi would be signed on 6 February 1840 and make New Zealand a British colony. In Australia, the young colonies were absorbing large numbers of assisted immigrants and the wool trade was becoming the export mainstay.

figures.name: Lin Zexu
figures.role: Qing imperial commissioner who confiscated and destroyed British opium at Humen in the summer of 1839

figures.name: Daoguang Emperor
figures.role: Reigning Qing emperor who authorised suppression of the opium trade and, after defeats, sued for peace

figures.name: Charles Elliot
figures.role: British Chief Superintendent of Trade at Canton whose surrender of the opium and subsequent Convention of Chuenpi triggered the war

figures.name: Lord Palmerston
figures.role: British foreign secretary who ordered the expeditionary force and dictated the peace terms

figures.name: Sir Henry Pottinger
figures.role: British plenipotentiary from 1841 who prosecuted the second campaign and negotiated the Treaty of Nanking

figures.name: William Gladstone
figures.role: Young Tory MP who denounced the war in the House of Commons on 8 April 1840

images.caption: The East India Company iron steamer Nemesis destroying Chinese war junks in Anson's Bay on 7 January 1841. Oil on canvas by Edward Duncan, 1843.

images.caption: Statue of Commissioner Lin Zexu in Fuzhou, Fujian province.

sources.title: The Opium War and the Opening of China
